meta.title: Social Communities and Influence Patterns in Super Mario Maker Player Networks
meta.subtitle: Using Network Science to Detect Player Communities, Identify Influencers, and Power Course Recommendations

items.secondary: ISSS606 - Applied Data Science for Social Networks
SMMNet - Super Mario Maker Social Network

items.primary: Do players form meaningful communities based on their gaming behavior?
Who are the core players, bridge players, and creators that shape the network structure?
Can network structure be used to support course recommendation and player discovery?

tabsContent.title: Five Core Entities
tabsContent.description: The SMMNet dataset is modelled around five interacting entities:

items.secondary: Baseline interaction between a player and a course

sections.heading: Filtering Criteria
sections.body: The bipartite player-course network is built using interactions with weight ≥ 3, restricted to the top 8,000 players and top 4,000 courses by activity.

sections.heading: Projection Threshold
sections.body: The bipartite graph is projected into a single-mode player-player network, keeping only edges where two players share ≥ 2 courses in common.

items.primary: Clean and standardized heterogeneous player_id and course ID across tables
Sampling only active players and highly popular levels
items.secondary: To ensure computational feasibility and eliminate network noise

sections.heading: Louvain vs Label Propagation
sections.body: An extended robustness check was run on the weighted player projection, comparing the Louvain algorithm against Label Propagation (LPA).

content.text: Higher modularity (0.3686) and a more useful community-size profile.
About 2.5x faster, but outcomes vary more across random seeds.
Use Louvain for the main community profiles; retain Label Propagation as the scalability benchmark.

sections.heading: Categorical Profile Validation
sections.body: Chi-square tests check whether community membership is associated with categorical course preferences. Difficulty mix: Cramer's V = 0.323 (chi2 = 30,230.1, p<0.001). Game style mix: Cramer's V = 0.127 (chi2 = 4,671.5, p<0.001). Difficulty is the stronger categorical separator; game style is still significant but weaker.

sections.heading: Numerical Profile Validation
sections.body: Kruskal-Wallis tests compare skewed numeric variables across the four core communities. Clear rate: Eta-squared(H) = 0.242 (H=23,381.7, p<0.001). Attempts: Eta-squared(H) = 0.181 (H=17,461.6, p<0.001). Stars received: Eta-squared(H) = 0.055 (H=5,315.8, p<0.001). All three numerical dimensions differ significantly; clear rate and attempts carry larger effects than stars.

tabsContent.title: Community Size and Density
tabsContent.note: Smaller groups are much denser - density captures cohesion, not popularity.

tabsContent.title: Community Behavioral Profiles
tabsContent.description: Labels are assigned after Louvain detection using dominant difficulty, game style, clear rate, average attempts, and average stars received by their courses.

tabsContent.title: Cross-Community Connection Strength
tabsContent.description: Rows show the source community; columns show where its connections go.

content.text: Diagonal shares range from 36.75% to 54.14%, so community membership is behaviorally meaningful.
The two casual communities exchange roughly 30% to 34% of their links.

Hardcore-to-Beginner links account for only 0.51%, indicating limited direct mixing. This implies recommendations can preserve relevance while deliberately adding a small amount of cross-community discovery.

tabsContent.title: Influence Roles: Who Shapes the Network

sections.heading: Creators
sections.body: Build new courses in Super Mario Maker and upload content to the entire social community.

sections.heading: Core Players
sections.body: Play and master courses, set trends, and develop new strategies and playthroughs.

sections.heading: Bridge Players
sections.body: Share content across communities and spread trends created by core players.

sections.heading: Normal Players
sections.body: Consume content, follow set trends, and engage socially.

tabsContent.title: Bridge vs Core vs Normal Players

sections.heading: Creators ∩ Bridge Players (n=11)
sections.body: Basilika, Conducteur59220, Feuxdelamoule, GTRP_Easy, Plusbellelavie5, WIIMoustique, coeur2017, cowboye123, indien1966, quentintheret, tc.coeur.

sections.heading: Bridge Players ∩ Core Players (n=9)
sections.body: Kodrak123, Mario1UPkoopa, NEWT82, RURI333HRIRU777H, Timridge, catmarioheidi, mario-tomoya, pedritopin2015, shimidos32.

sections.heading: Creators ∩ Core Players (n=0)
sections.body: No players occupy both the Creator and Core Player roles simultaneously - these two influence types are structurally distinct.

sections.heading: Player Similarity from Shared Course Behavior
sections.body: IDF weighting gives more credit to rare shared courses than globally popular ones. Most ties are weak (most player pairs share only a small amount of distinctive behavior), but a strong tail exists where a small set of pairs share many courses and receive high similarity weights. Strong neighbors become evidence for unseen courses, not automatic friends.

sections.heading: Recommendation as a Network Path
sections.body: A recommendation is explained as: target player → similar players → unseen courses. The path is auditable - each recommended course can be traced back to the neighbors who supported it.

sections.heading: Scoring Formula
sections.body: score(target, course) = sum(similarity x interaction weight x novelty). More support often helps, but strong neighbors and rare courses can outweigh raw count.

items.title: Similarity-Based Recommendations Win the Offline Comparison

content.text: Evaluated on 3,654 players for a Top-10 recommendation task. Similarity improves relevance while exposing a much broader and more diverse course catalog.
Restricting the analysis to top players and courses may not fully represent the entire player base.
The network represents a snapshot in time and does not capture how communities and influence evolve.
Recommendation performance was assessed offline; results have not been validated through live A/B testing with real players.

items.primary: Barabási, A.-L. (2016). Network Science. Cambridge University Press.
Blondel, V. D., Guillaume, J.-L., Lambiotte, R., & Lefebvre, E. (2008). Fast unfolding of communities in large networks. Journal of Statistical Mechanics: Theory and Experiment.
Moraes, L. M. P., & Cordeiro, R. L. F. (2019). SMMnet: A Social Network of Games Dataset. Brazilian Symposium on Databases, Dataset Showcase Workshop.
Moraes, L. M. P., & Cordeiro, R. L. F. (2025). Modeling and analyzing Social Networks of Games. Expert Systems with Applications.
